result.question:
How to minimize wall-clock time for (A) on-policy self-distillation DATA CAPTURE and (B) draft-head TRAINING, when adapting a DeepSeek MTP/DSpark speculative-decoding draft head to a REAP-pruned, NVFP4-quantized ~180B MoE target on a SINGLE 128GB unified-memory device (Jetson Thor, ~19 tok/s single-stream decode, the 96GiB weights are single-tenant so nothing else can hold the model concurrently).

The training is OFFLINE: the modified (REAP+NVFP4) target must generate the training responses on-policy (self-distillation), and we cache what the head needs; then we train only the ~3%-trainable head (which is itself an MLA + 256-expert top-6 MoE MTP layer) for 1-3 warm-started epochs. Our pre-flight estimate is capture-dominated (~16-70 hrs capture + a few-20 hrs train). We want to cut both.

Research and synthesize, with citations and concrete numbers where they exist:

CAPTURE (the bottleneck):
1. Throughput-oriented generation on a single device: how much does continuous/dynamic batching raise aggregate tok/s for a large MoE vs single-stream latency-optimized decode? Concrete batch-size vs throughput curves for MoE inference (vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM). Memory-bandwidth vs compute crossover for MoE decode.
2. LOSSLESS speculative decoding to accelerate the CAPTURE generation itself: since accepted tokens are exactly the target's distribution, can we use the unfine-tuned draft head (or any draft) to speed the target's own generation while capturing training data for that same head — i.e. bootstrap? Any precedent for spec-decoding the data-generation pass. Does using MTP/multi-token prediction during capture preserve the exact sampled distribution?
3. What MUST be captured for MTP/EAGLE-3-style TOKEN cross-entropy + logit-KD training: is it tokens only, or tokens + top-k target logits (what k is enough — 8/16/32?), or also last hidden states? EAGLE-3 abandoned feature/hidden-state regression — does that mean hidden-state capture can be SKIPPED, cutting capture compute/storage/IO? Storage footprint per token for each option.
4. Is FULL on-policy generation necessary, or does a HYBRID (existing instruction corpus + target-REGENERATED responses, or partial/prefix regeneration, temperature-0.8 regeneration a la SpecForge) capture most of the distribution-alignment benefit at a fraction of the generation cost? Disclosed ablations on self-generated vs regenerated vs static data.
5. Minimum data to converge a WARM-STARTED draft head: fewest tokens/examples that still recover acceptance (so we generate less). Any token-count scaling curves.

TRAINING:
6. Cache-once-train-many: precompute/cache target outputs once, then train the head over many epochs without re-running the 96GiB target. Storage vs recompute tradeoff; how EAGLE/SpecForge/FastMTP structure the offline cache.
7. Fastest head-training recipe: disclosed single-node wall-times (FastMTP <1 day on one H20), batch size, epochs, LoRA-on-head vs full-head fine-tune (does LoRA cut time without hurting acceptance?), efficient training of a small MoE head (only active experts get gradients).
8. Any 2025-2026 tooling specifically for accelerating speculative-decoding / draft-head data pipelines and training on a single or edge device (SpecForge offline mode, vLLM 'speculators', NeMo EAGLE recipes, on-device training).

Prioritize concrete, implementable levers with real numbers over generalities. Deliverable feeds a plan to cut a ~1-4 day capture+train run to as short as possible on one Jetson Thor.
result.summary: Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 13 verified claims unmerged.

confirmed.claim: As batch size grows, an MoE's effective compute converges from behaving like its small active-parameter model toward its full dense-parameter equivalent because activated parameters increase with batch size — i.e. the throughput benefit of batching an MoE saturates far earlier than for a dense model.
confirmed.quote: MoE performance degrades from being similar to 4B model to convering to 32B dense model as batch size increases. (c) Root cause is the increase in activated parameters at larger batches.

confirmed.claim: Module-based batching (accumulating tokens in host memory and dynamically launching large per-module batches for attention and experts) yields 8-31x higher decoding throughput than FlexGen/MoE-Lightning/DeepSpeed on a single GPU, and 21-63x on Mixtral-8x22B; e.g. Mixtral-8x7B decode reaches 469 tok/s vs 33 tok/s for FlexGen.
confirmed.quote: 8–31× higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art systems ... On ChatBot-Arena with Mixtral-8x22B: 21–63× improvement ... Decoding throughput on Mixtral-8x7B: 469 tokens/sec vs. 33 tokens/sec (FlexGen)

confirmed.claim: Large batching is what raises aggregate MoE decode throughput: baselines run only 0.3-0.4 tokens per expert at 0.1% GPU utilization, while MoE-Gen accumulates 75 tokens per expert at 41% utilization during decoding (8,192 tokens at 100% utilization during prefill).
confirmed.quote: Decoding: 75 tokens per expert (41% utilization) ... Compared to baselines: 0.3-0.4 tokens per expert with 0.1% utilization

confirmed.claim: On a 236B MoE (DeepSeek-V2-236B) single-GPU decode, batching-oriented MoE-Gen reaches 31 tok/s versus 0.8 tok/s for vLLM and 1 tok/s for DeepSpeed, roughly a 30-40x aggregate-throughput gain for dataset generation on one device.
confirmed.quote: On DeepSeek-V2-236B (decoding, 256-token output): vLLM: 0.8 tokens/sec ... MoE-Gen(G): 31 tokens/sec

confirmed.claim: For sparse MoE decode, arithmetic intensity scales as I ≈ n(Nk/Ne), i.e. it rises with batch size n and the fraction of experts activated (Nk/Ne), meaning larger batches move MoE decode from memory-bandwidth-bound toward compute-bound.

Batching dramatically raises aggregate throughput for LLM decode on a single GPU: vLLM on an A30 scales from 318.6 TPS at batch=16 to 964.4 TPS at batch=128, and SGLang on an A10 reaches 648.5 TPS at batch=128 vs 155-180 TPS at batch=16 — roughly a 3-4x aggregate gain from batch 16 to 128, on top of the gain over single-stream.
confirmed.quote: We scale the throughput evaluation under increasing batch sizes B ∈ {16, 32, 64, 128}. Table 4 reports offline throughput scaling as batch size increases. All engines benefit from larger batches... On the high-end A30, vLLM takes the lead, peaking at 964.4 TPS for B=128, which points to runtime optimisations that better exploit tensor cores and high-bandwidth memory.

confirmed.claim: Single-stream decode is far slower than batched aggregate throughput: the best single-stream TPOT is ~0.09 s/token (LMDeploy on L4) to ~0.095 s/token on A30 — i.e. ~10-11 tokens/s per stream — whereas offline batch throughput on the same-tier A30 reaches 964 TPS aggregate, implying batching multiplies capture throughput by roughly an order of magnitude or more.
confirmed.quote: LMDeploy again leads, recording ∼0.09 s/token on L4 and ∼0.095 s/token on A30, with vLLM and SGLang following closely and TGI lagging at both higher TPOT and higher variance.

confirmed.claim: MoE decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, and increasing batch size (or micro-batch size) raises the FFN's operational intensity because a larger batch means more computation per weight access, pushing the kernel from the memory-bound region toward the compute-bound crossover on the roofline — the mechanistic basis for batching to raise MoE throughput.
confirmed.quote: FFN’s operational intensity will increase as batch size or micro-batch size increases ... since, intuitively, a larger batch size means more computation per weight access.

confirmed.claim: High-throughput batched MoE inference is explicitly framed as the tool for offline, batch-processing workloads including synthetic data generation, where higher inference throughput translates directly into lower total completion time — exactly the regime of self-distillation data capture.
confirmed.quote: We are focusing on off-line, batch-processing workloads such as model evaluation [29], synthetic data generation [14], data wrangling [33], form processing [7], and LLM for relational analytics [31] where higher inference throughput translates into lower total completion time.

confirmed.claim: Speculative decoding accelerates on-policy generation without altering the target sampling distribution — the rejection procedure keeps outputs on the target/verifier policy, making it lossless for on-policy data capture.
confirmed.quote: speculative decoding raises throughput without changing the sampling distribution the RL signal depends on

confirmed.claim: At 8B scale, spec decoding cut rollout (on-policy generation) latency 1.8x on RL-Zero (100.0s to 56.6s/step) and 1.5x on RL-Think (133.6s to 87.0s/step), with overall step speedups of 1.4x and 1.3x.
confirmed.quote: rollout generation latency decreases from 100.0s to 56.6s per step, a 1.8x speedup

confirmed.claim: Draft speculation length k has an optimum (k=3 sweet spot); too-large k (k>=5 on reasoning workloads) is slower than plain autoregressive decoding, and optimal k depends on model size, deployment scale, and per-instance batch size.
confirmed.quote: k=3 is the sweet spot; k≥5 on RL-Think is actually slower than autoregressive decoding

confirmed.claim: Speculative decoding can losslessly accelerate the token-by-token generation (rollout) pass used to produce training data, cutting generation wall-clock time by up to 50% while preserving the exact model outputs and identical training curves.
confirmed.quote: we propose DAS, a Distribution Aware Speculative decoding framework that accelerates RL rollouts without altering model outputs ... DAS reduces rollout time up to 50% while preserving identical training curves

refuted.claim: MoE decode at the moderate batch sizes imposed by latency SLOs is memory-bandwidth-bound, with latency scaling directly with the number of distinct experts that must be loaded from HBM — so batching quickly negates MoE sparsity because a small batch already forces loading nearly all experts.

Batching-oriented MoE serving (MoE-Lens) achieves on average 4.6x and up to 25.5x higher throughput than the prior state-of-the-art throughput system MoE-Lightning, demonstrating large aggregate tok/s gains from throughput-optimized (batched) MoE decode over latency-oriented approaches.

Throughput-oriented batched MoE inference with weight offloading achieves up to 10.3x higher aggregate token throughput than state-of-the-art offloading LLM systems on a single memory-constrained GPU (Mixtral 8x7B on one 16GB T4), demonstrating that continuous/dynamic batching dramatically raises aggregate tok/s for large MoE decode versus latency-optimized single-stream serving.

unverified.claim: Because accepted tokens follow the target's distribution, distribution-aware speculative decoding accelerates the data-generation pass without compromising learning quality (the captured data is distributionally identical to non-speculative generation).

EAGLE-3 abandons the feature/hidden-state regression loss entirely and trains the draft head with direct token prediction (token cross-entropy) via a 'training-time test' that simulates multi-step generation during training.

Despite dropping the feature-prediction LOSS, EAGLE-3 still consumes hidden states as INPUT: it fuses low-, mid-, and high-layer target features (concatenated into 3k-dim vectors, reduced to k-dim), so hidden-state capture cannot be fully skipped for EAGLE-3-style training.
EAGLE-3 draft-head training data is generated on-policy by calling the target model to produce responses rather than using a fixed/static dataset.

On gpt-oss-120b with ShareGPT 100k, SpecForge's offline EAGLE-3 pipeline spends 6.16 hr on prefill (data capture) plus 9.16 hr on training (15.32 hr total); reusing inference-time hidden states eliminates the prefill/capture phase entirely, giving 9.16 hr total and a 1.67x end-to-end speedup over offline (and 3.02x over online recompute).

Caching all EAGLE-3 target hidden states for a full training corpus is storage-heavy: SpecForge offline stores 4.66 TB for gpt-oss-120b (up to 46.40 TB for Llama-3.3-70B), whereas keeping only a small active-batch buffer needs 0.19 TB (down to 0.55-1.92 TB for other models).

The alternative to caching once - regenerating target hidden states on-demand each training step (SpecForge 'online') - triples prefill/capture cost (18.48 hr vs 6.16 hr) and drives end-to-end speedup below 1.0 (0.55x), because it must reload the target and re-run prefill every step; this quantifies the cache-once vs recompute tradeoff.

vLLM's Speculators v0.3.0 offline data pipeline captures per-token input_ids, intercepted intermediate hidden_states from the target's prefill forward pass, and a binary loss_mask, saved as individual .pt files (plus a token_freq.pt for vocab mapping) — i.e. hidden states ARE cached for EAGLE-3-style training.

The offline architecture separates data generation from training so cached hidden states can be reused across training runs (cache-once, train-many), though the article does not explicitly quantify this.
